column1: Maria enjoys counting numbers! Adding numbers is fun! One plus one equals two, and two plus two equals four, and three plus three equals six. But what about four plus four? Maria asked her mom. Her mom said that four plus four equals eight. "I see!" Maria said, "I'm 4 years old now. In 4 more years, I will be 8!" Maria is a quick learner!

Little Maria loves counting numbers! She enjoys adding them together. Adding numbers is simple, and she knows that:

1 + 1 = 2
2 + 2 = 4
3 + 3 = 6

But there was one thing she didn't know: What happens when you add 4 and 4? Curious, she asked her mom. Her mom told Maria that 4 + 4 equals 8! "Oh, I understand now!" Maria exclaimed. "I am already four years old. In four more years, I will be eight!" Maria is a quick learner! She's not a slow learner.

Maria is smart and learns fast!

Bob bought an old house that needed fix-ups. The house had been cheap because it was in a neighborhood with a history of trouble. The previous owners lost the house to foreclosure, and some people bought it and made it look nice. But when Bob moved in, he found that many changes were just on the outside.

The kitchen appliances were new, but the electrical work was old and caused blackouts whenever the dishwasher or coffee machine ran. Bob spent over a thousand dollars to fix the electricity. The carpets put down by the house flippers covered up hardwood floors that Bob decided to refinish for $3,000 more.

The house got very hot in summer and cold in winter, so the windows were cheap and didn't help. Bob spent more money on new double-pane storm windows and ceiling fans. He also added solar panels to make the house energy efficient, which saved money on electric bills and increased the home's value.

In just two years, the changes Bob made cost over $20,000. By that time, his house was worth almost $100,000 more than what he paid for it. Bob felt happy because when he sold the house, he would get back a lot of money. He knew this investment would be profitable.

At a wedding reception, there's often a host called the Master of Ceremonies (MC) who keeps things running smoothly by making announcements. For instance, the MC might let everyone know when the newlywed couple arrives, and everyone stands up to applaud. Typically, music is played for dancing, which could come from a DJ or live band that may even take requests. When the new couple enters, they usually dance their first dance together to a song they picked beforehand, often something romantic symbolizing their love. Sometimes the bride dances with her father and the groom with his mother.

Food at weddings can be served buffet-style or as a sit-down meal. If it's a buffet, guests serve themselves. When responding to wedding invitations, people usually decide whether they want chicken, fish, or meat. Guests don't often get much time with the bride and groom because they're busy greeting everyone. However, the newlyweds always manage to enjoy a piece of the wedding cake together. Traditionally, they cut it in half and feed each other a bite.

Miami is renowned for its tropical climate, retiree community, and Cuban-American population, but not as an international art center. Fortunately for downtown Miami, real estate tycoon Jorge Pérez aims to change that.

Pérez, who has a penchant for both real estate development and art, donated $40 million, half from his personal art collection, to the Pérez Art Museum Miami (PAMM), one of few major institutions in the United States with a Hispanic name. This move could certainly put Miami on the global art map.

The downtown area is undergoing significant revitalization: David Beckham intends to construct a stadium there for his newly announced Miami soccer team. Within the art world, the annual international art and fashion extravaganza Art Basel, held in Miami Beach for over a decade, is considered the nation's leading art fair, attracting tens of thousands of people to the five-day event.

Pérez hopes his museum will elevate Miami to new artistic heights. Cultural institutions have played a crucial role in Miami's growth, with Pérez Art Museum serving as a prime example, according to Pérez's statements to CNN.

Before becoming known as the Condo King of South Florida, Pérez began his career as an urban planner, focusing on low-income housing developments. He later co-founded a real estate development firm, The Related Group of Florida, and delved into high-end condo construction in the 1980s.

In 2005, TIME magazine named Pérez one of the top 25 most influential Hispanics in the United States. He has also been featured on Forbes' list of 400 richest Americans several times, making him the only Latino immigrant to do so. Born in Argentina to Cuban parents, Pérez grew up in Colombia before moving to Miami as a teenager. His lifelong passion for Latin American art stems from his upbringing.

In his early 20s, he started collecting paintings by Roberto Matta, Diego Rivera, Wifredo Lam, and Joaquin Torres-Garcia. Now, 110 works selected by the museum from Pérez's private collection are available for public viewing within the three-story, 200,000-square-foot museum designed by Pritzker Prize-winning Swiss architects Herzog & de Meuron.

Upon entering the $220 million building, visitors are greeted by tall glass windows claimed to be the world's largest hurricane impact resistant panes. Hanging from every side of the museum are 70 self-watering hydroponic gardens designed by French landscape artist Patrick Blanc.

The museum primarily showcases international art from the 20th and 21st centuries, and is currently exhibiting Chinese artist-activist Ai Weiwei's According to What? collection. PAMM serves as one of the main attractions in Museum Park, which will also house the Patricia and Phillip Frost Museum of Science, set to open in 2015.

The Pérez Art Museum faced controversy upon its naming, with some board members resigning in protest due to concerns that the name did not adequately represent the city's central art museum. However, Pérez was involved with the museum long before the building project began and expressed gratitude for being recognized by the board in this manner.

Despite the controversy surrounding naming rights, the Pérez Art Museum has reached the 90% mark in its $220 million capital campaign.

It's New Year's Day! A boy lived in a place called Las Vegas. He drove to a big building called a casino. He walked to a game called roulette. He bought one chip for $100. He tossed a coin up high. He caught the coin. It was heads! So, he placed his $100 chip on a red spot. The dealer spun the wheel. Round and round it went! The dealer then pushed a silver ball. Around and around it went! The wheel stopped turning. The ball stopped too. It landed in a red slot! He won! The dealer gave him another $100 chip. He thanked the dealer with a $10 tip. He traded both chips for money. He went home. He felt very happy. He thought this would be a great year!

The wheel and ball stopped. The ball landed in a red slot. He won! The dealer gave him another $100 chip. He thanked the dealer with a $10 gift. He got all his chips from the dealer. He went home, feeling happy. It's going to be a wonderful year!

Miami is renowned for its tropical climate, retirement community, and Cuban-American population, but not so much as an international art hub. However, this could soon change due to the efforts of billionaire real estate developer Jorge Pérez.

Pérez, who is also an art enthusiast, donated $40 million, half of which came from his personal art collection, to the Pérez Art Museum Miami (PAMM), one of the few major institutions in the United States with a Hispanic name. This move aims to establish Miami as a significant player on the global art scene.

The city of Miami is currently undergoing a significant revitalization. Soccer legend David Beckham plans to build a stadium there for his newly announced Miami soccer team. Within the art world, the international art and fashion event Art Basel, which has been held in Miami Beach for over a decade, is considered the country's leading art fair, attracting tens of thousands of people over five days.

Jorge Pérez hopes that his museum will help take Miami to the next level in terms of its cultural offerings. He stated in an interview with CNN that cultural institutions have played a critical role in the growth of Miami, with this museum serving as a prime example.

Before becoming known as the "Condo King" of South Florida, Pérez began his career as an urban planner, focusing on low-income housing developments. He later co-founded a real estate development firm, The Related Group of Florida, and worked in high-end condo construction in the 1980s.

In 2005, TIME magazine named him one of the top 25 most influential Hispanics in the United States. He has also been listed multiple times on Forbes' list of 400 richest Americans, making him the only Latino immigrant to do so.

Born in Argentina to Cuban parents, Pérez grew up in Colombia before moving to Miami as a teenager. His lifelong passion for Latin American art stems from his upbringing. In his early 20s, he began collecting paintings by Roberto Matta, Diego Rivera, Wifredo Lam, and Joaquin Torres-Garcia. Now, 110 works selected from Pérez's private collection are on display at the three-story, 200,000-square-foot museum designed by Pritzker Prize-winning Swiss architects Herzog & de Meuron.

The $220 million building features tall glass windows said to be the largest hurricane impact resistant in the world and 70 self-watering hydroponic gardens designed by French landscape artist Patrick Blanc. The museum focuses on international art of the 20th and 21st centuries and is currently showing Chinese artist-activist Ai Weiwei's According to What? collection.

PAMM is one of the main attractions in what was known as Bicentennial Park, now Museum Park, which will also house the Patricia and Phillip Frost Museum of Science, set to open in 2015. The museum faced some controversy when it was named after its donor, but Pérez has been involved with the project for many years and expressed pride at being recognized by the board. Despite the initial opposition, he believes that most people want to be remembered for their contributions to worthy causes.

The name change of the Miami Science Museum to the Patricia and Phillip Frost Museum of Science after a generous donor was well received by the community, demonstrating that naming rights are not uncommon in American art museums. While some people may disagree on a name, everyone can see that downtown Miami is becoming a hub for science and art, which means donations are needed to continue its growth. So far, PAMM has reached the 90% mark in its $220 million capital campaign.

Absolutely! You've captured the essence of soccer very well. Here are a few additional details about the game:

1. **Field Dimensions**: A standard soccer field (or pitch) is rectangular and can vary in size, but for international matches, it measures between 100-130 yards (90-120 meters) in length and 50-100 yards (45-90 meters) in width.

2. **Goals**: Each goal is typically 8 feet (2.4 meters) high and 8 yards (7.32 meters) wide. The crossbar, which separates the two upright posts, is usually about 10 feet (3 meters) above the ground.

3. **Duration of the Game**: A soccer match consists of two halves, each lasting 45 minutes. There's also a 15-minute break between halves known as halftime. If the score is tied at the end of regular time, extra time (two periods of 15 minutes) or penalty shootouts may be used to determine the winner.

4. **Number of Players**: Each team has eleven players on the field, one of whom is the goalkeeper. The goalkeeper's primary role is to prevent the opposing team from scoring by blocking shots and collecting the ball. Substitutions are allowed during the game, up to a maximum of three per team.

5. **Scoring**: A goal is scored when the ball completely crosses the goal line between the goalposts and under the crossbar, as controlled or kicked by any part of the attacking team (except the goalkeeper). A goal scores one point for the team that made it. The team with the most goals at the end of the match wins.

6. **Offside Rule**: One unique aspect of soccer is the offside rule, which aims to discourage players from having an unfair advantage over their opponents when attacking the goal. A player is in an offside position if they are nearer to their opponent's goal than both the ball and the second-last defender (a teammate of the opponent who last played the ball) when the opposing team plays the ball, unless they are level with or beyond the second-last defender.

Title Announcement for a New TV Show: Fear the Soccer Game

Exciting news! The much-anticipated companion series to AMC's popular show, The Walking Dead, now has an official title. It will be called Fear the Soccer Game.

The announcement was made last Friday by executive producer Robert Kirkman. This spinoff starts as a prequel to the original show and will delve into the world of soccer.

Throughout its development season, the series went by code names like Cobalt and Fear the Soccer Game before finally being picked up for a full series run and preemptively renewed for a second season. On March 9th, AMC officially announced the pickup and renewal of this yet-to-be-named series.

Robert Kirkman, creator of The Walking Dead comic series, co-wrote the pilot with Dave Erickson from Sons of Anarchy. Kirkman and Erickson will serve as executive producers alongside Gale Anne Hurd and Dave Alpert from The Walking Dead. Erickson will also be the showrunner for Fear the Soccer Game, while Adam Davidson directs the pilot. Veteran visual effects guru Greg Nicotero from The Walking Dead is also attached to the series as an executive producer.

Although AMC has kept the plot details under wraps, sources tell The Hollywood Reporter that the drama unfolds in Los Angeles during the beginning of a global soccer phenomenon. While AMC confirms that the companion series takes place in Los Angeles, they have revealed little else beyond confirming it will focus on new characters and storylines.

Cliff Curtis, known for Gang Related, stars as Sean Cabrera, a teacher who shares a son with his ex-wife. Kim Dickens from Sons of Anarchy is set to co-star as Nancy, a guidance counselor who works alongside Sean and has a romantic relationship with him. Frank Dillane, famous for Harry Potter and the Half-Blood Prince, plays Nick, Nancy's troubled son battling substance abuse issues. Alycia Debnam Carey, from Into the Woods, portrays Ashley, Nancy's ambitious daughter who dreams of escaping Los Angeles for Berkeley when chaos ensues.

Fear the Soccer Game will premiere in late summer, with a second season planned for 2016. The exact premiere date has not been revealed yet. The series finale of the flagship series airs Sunday at 9 p.m.

Absolutely! Soccer, also known as football in many parts of the world, is indeed a thrilling sport that brings together people from various cultures and backgrounds. The strategic teamwork, intense physicality, and unpredictable moments make it an exciting spectacle to watch and play.

The basic objective of the game is to score goals by getting the ball into the opposing team's net. A match consists of two 45-minute halves with a 15-minute break in between, making up a total of 90 minutes. If the scores are tied at the end of regular time, extra time or penalty shootouts may be employed to determine the winner.

Players have to maintain constant movement and communication while working together to possess the ball and create scoring opportunities. They must also defend their own goal from attacks by the opposing team. Fouls committed by players can lead to free kicks, penalties, or even sending-off, which means the offending player is temporarily or permanently removed from the game.

Soccer has a rich history and is played at various levels – from amateur leagues to professional tournaments like the FIFA World Cup, UEFA Champions League, and several national leagues around the world. Its popularity is evidenced by the passionate fan bases that support their favorite teams and players, making soccer a truly global phenomenon.

Miami aspires to be recognized not just for its tropical weather, retiree community, and Cuban-American population, but also as a significant international art hub. This transformation is spearheaded by Jorge Pérez, a billionaire real estate developer with a passion for art.

Pérez donated $40 million towards the Pérez Art Museum Miami (PAMM), one of the few major institutions in the United States with a Hispanic name. With half of this sum derived from his private art collection, he aims to elevate Miami's cultural landscape significantly.

The museum, a three-story, 200,000-square-foot structure designed by Pritzker Prize-winning Swiss architects Herzog & de Meuron, showcases over 100 works from Pérez's private collection. The building boasts tall glass windows claimed to be the largest hurricane impact resistant in the world and 70 self-watering hydroponic gardens designed by French landscape artist Patrick Blanc.

PAMM houses various international art of the 20th and 21st centuries, currently exhibiting Chinese artist-activist Ai Weiwei's According to What? collection. The museum is one of the main attractions in Museum Park, which will also house the Patricia and Phillip Frost Museum of Science scheduled to open in 2015.

Despite some controversy surrounding the naming of the museum after its donor, Pérez has been deeply involved with the project since its inception and feels honored by the recognition. Controversy or not, the city's art scene is growing, and Pérez's contribution marks a significant milestone in Miami's artistic evolution.

The players engage in continuous movement, often sliding on grass, colliding, and running back and forth in an effort to outscore their opponents and secure victory.

At a wedding, there are various kinds of fun. Often, an MC (Master of Ceremonies) helps keep things running smoothly by making announcements about what will happen next. For example, the MC may tell everyone when the newlywed couple enters the reception hall and everyone stands up to applaud them. Music is usually played at a wedding reception, and people are expected to dance. The music can come from a DJ or a live band. Sometimes, guests can request songs for the DJ or live band to play. When the newlyweds enter the reception, they often dance together first to a song they chose beforehand. This is usually a romantic song that represents their love for each other. Sometimes, the bride dances with her father and the groom with his mother. After these special dances, everyone else is invited to join on the dance floor.

At a wedding reception, food can be served in different ways. One way is called buffet style, where people serve themselves. Another way is a sit-down meal, where waiters bring the food to you. Guests usually choose what they want to eat when they respond to their wedding invitation. During a wedding, guests don't spend much time with the bride and groom because they are busy meeting everyone. The newlyweds might not even get a chance to eat. However, almost every newlywed couple gets to enjoy their wedding cake. The bride and groom usually cut through the cake together, then feed each other a bite.

It sounds like you are quite determined to find resources for your research! Given your concerns about travel expenses and distance, I suggest we explore online options first. Many libraries offer digital collections that you can access from home. You might want to check out WorldCat (worldcat.org) or Google Scholar (scholar.google.com) to see if they have any books on your subject available in digital format.

2. Renting or Purchasing: You could consider renting or buying used copies of the books online. Websites like Abebooks (abebooks.com) and ThriftBooks (thriftbooks.com) offer a wide selection of used books for reasonable prices. This would be an upfront cost, but you'd have the physical books in your possession to study.

3. Research Institutions: Universities or research institutions may have libraries that specialize in specific subjects and are open to the public. You might want to check if there are any nearby and see if they carry the books you need.

4. Ask colleagues/friends: Reach out to others in your field who might have copies of the books you're looking for. They may be willing to lend or share resources with you.

The music might come from a DJ or a live band. Sometimes, they'll play songs that guests request. When the couple arrives, they often share their first dance together to a special song. This song is usually romantic and symbolizes their love for each other. Sometimes the bride dances with her father and the groom with his mother. After these special dances, everyone is invited to dance on the floor.

Food at weddings can be served in two ways – buffet style or as a sit-down meal served by waiters. Guests choose between dishes like meat, chicken, or fish when they respond to the wedding invitation. The couple traditionally cuts the cake together and feeds each other a bite.

Brian and his friends went to the Hollywood 18 where many skaters make videos. Jumping those stairs would make him famous with his friends! As Brian approached the stairs, he counted them one by one, making sure they had more than 15 steps. On his first try, he fell off his board after trying to jump over the stairs. He was scared but tried again. This time, he jumped over some stairs... just not quite enough to pass the 16th step! He landed on the ground, a little bumped but okay.

Two girls watched Brian and asked if he could try again. Determined to succeed, Brian took another shot. As he prepared for his jump, he could see the girls who had gathered to watch him. He took deep breaths, ready to do it. It was now or never! With a push from the pavement, he pedaled faster and faster, reaching the top of the stairs. Then, he jumped!

For about 5 seconds, Brian flew through the air before landing safely on his skateboard. The girls cheered for him, and he stopped to take it all in. He had finally beaten his record! Excited, the girls ran over to give him a hug, but they kept their distance because he smelled funny. Everyone started laughing and congratulated Brian. What an amazing day it was!

At 6 in the morning, a noise wakes up Sandy. It's her alarm clock! Sandy gets out of bed and takes a quick shower. After that, she makes breakfast. She uses a clean bowl from the dishwasher and puts an apple cinnamon oatmeal packet inside. Then she adds milk, stirs it, and warms it up in the microwave. While waiting, she gets dressed for the day.

Now fully dressed, Sandy takes her oatmeal out of the microwave. It's cool enough to eat now. She enjoys each bite because it's her favorite flavor! This reminds her of her mother who always made sure she ate a warm breakfast before leaving home in the morning.

Sandy checks the time on her phone and swallows her oatmeal quickly. Then she goes outside where the wind blows her hair back. She covers her head with her coat's hood, wraps up tightly with her scarf, and braces herself for her walk to the train station.

Once seated on the train, memories of her mother come back again. So, she texts: "Hi mom, thanks for the warm scarf you knitted for me! It helped me today. Love always."

At a wedding, food can be served in a buffet style where people serve themselves or it could be a sit-down meal brought by waiters. Guests choose between having meat, chicken, or fish. They make their choices when they reply to the wedding invitation.

Similar trends are observed in Canada, Spain, France, Germany, and Japan, where Facebook has significant followings. Despite these losses, Facebook is still growing in South America, particularly in Brazil and India, which saw a 6% and 4% increase respectively.

The decline in developed markets raises concerns about the platform's future, with some analysts comparing it to the fate of MySpace. The shift towards mobile usage might not be enough to compensate for this loss, as smartphone minutes have doubled but desktop usage is declining rapidly.

Facebook has taken steps to appeal to smartphone users, such as introducing Facebook Home, a software that delivers news and photos from friends directly to the locked home screen of certain Android phones. The company has also acknowledged the potential loss of younger users to other mobile-centric services in recent stock market filings.

Investors will be keen to learn more about Facebook's mobile user base growth and advertising revenues when the company reports its first quarter performance. Wall Street expects revenues of about $1.44 billion, up from $1.06 billion in 2012.
